briefing.headline: 11 high-priority items in the latest EM scan
briefing.summary: Top sources today: European Journal of Emergency Medicine, MedPage Today Emergency Medicine, REBEL EM. Main themes: trials, point-of-care ultrasound, guideline or formal recommendation. Best early reads: Association between patient-to-nurse ratios in emergency departments and patient, staff, and organizational outcomes: a systematic review; Whole Blood Not Better Than Components En Route to Hospital After Traumatic Injury.
briefing.editorialHeadline: Trauma Resuscitation & Targeted Interventions: What's Changing at the Bedside
briefing.editorialSummary:
The evidence base continues to refine our approach to high-acuity resuscitation. In trauma, the TOWAR data offers a compelling argument that whole, type O blood en route may be a viable, perhaps simpler, alternative to full component therapy when managing hemorrhagic shock. Meanwhile, for pulmonary embolism, the literature is pushing toward more targeted lysis; the HI-PEITHO trial suggests that adding ultrasound-guided, catheter-directed fibrinolysis to standard anticoagulation may specifically benefit the sickest intermediate-risk patients by mitigating short-term hemodynamic dips.

Beyond massive transfusion, we see refinement in procedural care. For routine vascular access in pediatrics, the use of liquid crystal vein locators appears to offer a tangible benefit in first-attempt success rates, potentially reducing both provider time and patient distress. Similarly, for acute rib fractures, the data supports integrating the Serratus Anterior Plane Block as a safe, effective adjunct to standard analgesia, offering a non-opioid edge.

These findings—from optimizing blood products in trauma to refining regional anesthesia for simple fractures—underscore a trend toward highly specific, evidence-backed interventions. Today’s reading set demands that we move beyond generalized protocols and critically assess which advanced tools or procedural tweaks offer the most measurable, actionable benefit for our most common, yet most complex, presentations.

dailyEditorial.title: Association between patient-to-nurse ratios in emergency departments and patient, staff, and organizational outcomes: a systematic review
dailyEditorial.takeaway: While the concept of setting nurse ratios sounds appealing for quality improvement, remember that this systematic review's key findings on ED mortality are not available in the provided text. Clinicians should approach the general literature suggesting ratio benefits with caution, recognizing that the ED environment may confound these associations. For now, the evidence synthesis is incomplete, so major changes based solely on this overview are premature.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This systematic review synthesized the existing literature to determine if establishing specific patient-to-nurse ratios in the emergency department (ED) is associated with improved patient mortality.
dailyEditorial.summary: This systematic review synthesized the existing literature to determine if establishing specific patient-to-nurse ratios in the emergency department (ED) is associated with improved patient mortality. While the general literature suggests that setting nurse ratios benefits both patients and staff in various care settings, the applicability of these findings specifically to the chaotic environment of the ED remains uncertain. The authors conducted a comprehensive search across major databases for studies published between 2004 and 2025, employing two independent reviewers for data extraction. The primary goal was to quantify the relationship between these staffing ratios and overall ED mortality. However, the provided excerpt only details the methodology, noting the search strategy and the rigorous review process, without presenting the actual findings regarding mortality.

dailyEditorial.title: Whole Blood Not Better Than Components En Route to Hospital After Traumatic Injury
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When managing a trauma patient at risk of hemorrhagic shock, administering whole, type O blood en route appears to be as effective as component therapy based on the TOWAR data. This suggests that whole blood can be a viable, potentially simpler, resuscitation choice at the point of care. However, always consider the local protocol and the specific clinical context, as this finding compares outcomes, not necessarily the optimal resuscitation strategy for every patient.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The TOWAR randomized trial provides data suggesting that for trauma patients anticipated to develop hemorrhagic shock, administering whole, type O blood en route to the hospital yields outcomes comparable to administeri…
dailyEditorial.summary: The TOWAR randomized trial provides data suggesting that for trauma patients anticipated to develop hemorrhagic shock, administering whole, type O blood en route to the hospital yields outcomes comparable to administering pre-transfusion blood components. This finding challenges the historical emphasis on component therapy for massive transfusion protocols in the acute trauma setting. The study's primary conclusion is that whole blood can serve as an acceptable alternative to component-based resuscitation in this high-risk population. This is a notable piece of evidence for guiding immediate pre-hospital or point-of-care transfusion decisions in trauma.

dailyEditorial.title: HI-PEITHO Trial: Ultrasound-facilitated, Catheter Directed Fibrinolysis for PE
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For intermediate-risk PE, standard anticoagulation remains the essential initial step. Consider US-CDT plus anticoagulation primarily for those patients exhibiting signs of significant hemodynamic instability, as this combination showed benefit in preventing short-term decompensation in the sickest group. Remember that this advanced therapy is not indicated for all intermediate-risk patients and requires careful risk stratification.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The HI-PEITHO trial was a randomized controlled trial designed to test whether adding ultrasound-facilitated, catheter-directed fibrinolysis (US-CDT) to standard anticoagulation improves outcomes in intermediate-risk pu…
dailyEditorial.summary: The HI-PEITHO trial was a randomized controlled trial designed to test whether adding ultrasound-facilitated, catheter-directed fibrinolysis (US-CDT) to standard anticoagulation improves outcomes in intermediate-risk pulmonary embolism (PE) patients. The primary question addressed was whether this combination therapy offers an advantage over anticoagulation alone in this specific cohort. The key takeaway from the analysis is that the addition of US-CDT appears to confer a benefit specifically in mitigating short-term hemodynamic decompensation among the sickest subset of intermediate-risk PE patients. Overall, the trial suggests a role for this advanced intervention in a carefully selected, high-risk subset, while maintaining anticoagulation as the cornerstone of management.

dailyEditorial.title: Effect of a liquid crystal vein locator on intravenous catheterization in young children: a randomized trial
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For routine PIVC in young children, incorporating a liquid crystal vein locator appears beneficial, as it correlates with higher first-attempt success and reduced procedural time. This could translate to less distress for the child and faster access for the provider. However, remember this is based on a randomized trial, and its utility should be weighed against the added equipment and time required for setup at the bedside.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This randomized trial assessed the utility of a liquid crystal vein locator during peripheral intravenous catheterization (PIVC) specifically in the pediatric population.
dailyEditorial.summary: This randomized trial assessed the utility of a liquid crystal vein locator during peripheral intravenous catheterization (PIVC) specifically in the pediatric population. The primary finding is that employing this locator significantly improved procedural metrics compared to standard techniques. Specifically, the group using the locator demonstrated a statistically significant increase in first-attempt success rates, required fewer total puncture attempts, and achieved shorter overall procedure times. These results suggest a tangible benefit to the efficiency and potentially the experience of the child during necessary vascular access in the emergency setting. Overall, the data points toward a practical advantage for this technology in pediatric emergency medicine.

dailyEditorial.title: Serratus Anterior Plane Block for Acute Rib Fractures in the Emergency Department: A Randomized Controlled Trial
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Given the significant improvement in PIC scores, consider adding SAPB to your standard analgesic regimen for acute rib fractures in the ED. Since the procedure was reported as safe and feasible for ED personnel, this offers a valuable, non-opioid adjunct. Remember that while promising, this is based on a single trial, so integrate it thoughtfully into your local protocols.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This randomized controlled trial evaluated the utility of adding a Serratus Anterior Plane Block (SAPB) to standard analgesic management for acute rib fractures encountered in the emergency department.
dailyEditorial.summary: This randomized controlled trial evaluated the utility of adding a Serratus Anterior Plane Block (SAPB) to standard analgesic management for acute rib fractures encountered in the emergency department. The primary finding was that incorporating the SAPB significantly improved Pain Intensity Control (PIC) scores among this patient cohort. The authors emphasize that the procedure was performed safely by emergency physicians, suggesting it is a practical and scalable adjunct therapy. Overall, the data supports the integration of this regional anesthetic technique into the routine ED armamentarium for managing rib fracture pain.

dailyEditorial.title: 2025 guideline updates for community-acquired pneumonia diagnosis and management
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When managing nonsevere CAP, remember to consider de-escalating antibiotic therapy to a minimum of three days for stable patients, which is a key update. Also, don't overlook the utility of lung ultrasound when you have an experienced reader available, as this is gaining formal support. However, be mindful that these guidelines are updated, and local institutional protocols should always be cross-referenced, especially regarding viral testing and antibiotic necessity.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The new 2025 American Thoracic Society guidelines provide an updated framework for diagnosing and managing community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) encountered in the emergency department.
dailyEditorial.summary: The new 2025 American Thoracic Society guidelines provide an updated framework for diagnosing and managing community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) encountered in the emergency department. These guidelines touch upon imaging modalities, antibiotic stewardship, and the role of corticosteroids. Notably, the recommendations appear to bolster the utility of lung ultrasound when performed by experienced clinicians, suggesting it's a valuable tool in the ED setting. Furthermore, there's a shift in antibiotic management, suggesting that for otherwise healthy patients who test positive for a virus, antibiotics may not be necessary. Clinically, the guidelines advocate for shortening the duration of antibiotic therapy, recommending a minimum course of three days for both stable outpatients and admitted patients with nonsevere CAP.

dailyEditorial.title: TOP: Liberal or restrictive post-op transfusion
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For high-risk patients post-major surgery, the data from TOP generally support a more restrictive approach to transfusion, suggesting that aiming for a hemoglobin target of 7 g/dL or higher may be safer than liberal transfusion practices. However, remember that the decision must be individualized, and the risk of bleeding versus the risk of transfusion-related complications needs careful weighing at the bedside.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The TOP trial addressed a common dilemma in high-risk surgical patients: whether to adopt a liberal or restrictive approach to managing postoperative anemia.
dailyEditorial.summary: The TOP trial addressed a common dilemma in high-risk surgical patients: whether to adopt a liberal or restrictive approach to managing postoperative anemia. Specifically, it compared a liberal transfusion threshold, defined as maintaining hemoglobin levels below 10 g/dL, against a more restrictive threshold of below 7 g/dL in adults with known cardiac risk following major surgery. The primary endpoint was the reduction of 90-day all-cause death or major ischemic events. While the overall trend in the literature suggests favoring more conservative transfusion practices, this trial provides a direct comparison in a vulnerable cohort, which is valuable for refining institutional protocols.

dailyEditorial.title: Catheter-Directed Fibrinolysis for Pulmonary Embolism: Is It Old NEWS?
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For acute PE management, consider ultrasound-guided, catheter-directed fibrinolysis as a targeted alternative to systemic thrombolytics, especially in patients with high bleeding risk. While the technique appears promising for localized clot lysis, remember that its utility should be weighed against the risks of procedural complications and the need for careful hemodynamic monitoring. Always review the institutional guidelines regarding appropriate patient selection for this advanced intervention.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This article details the use of ultrasound-guided, catheter-directed fibrinolysis as a treatment strategy for acute pulmonary embolism.
dailyEditorial.summary: This article details the use of ultrasound-guided, catheter-directed fibrinolysis as a treatment strategy for acute pulmonary embolism. The authors present data on this minimally invasive approach, which aims to directly administer thrombolytic agents to the pulmonary embolus site. The technique utilizes ultrasound guidance to improve visualization and placement accuracy of the catheter within the pulmonary vasculature. This represents a more targeted intervention compared to systemic thrombolysis, which carries a higher risk of bleeding complications. The findings suggest this method offers a potentially safer and more localized means of achieving clot lysis in PE patients.

dailyEditorial.title: Modified brain injury guidelines in a regional Australian setting indicate significant resource savings but also safety concerns
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When considering adopting TBI stratification guidelines like mBIG, remember that while resource savings are appealing, the evidence base is foreign to your local practice. Do not implement these pathways based solely on US data; instead, critically evaluate the local resource utilization patterns and the specific criteria for escalation to ensure safety is maintained. Be cautious about making sweeping changes without local validation.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: Traumatic brain injury remains a significant burden in the emergency department, with resource utilization—including unnecessary neurosurgical consultations and repeat CTs—being a major concern even though most injuries…
dailyEditorial.summary: Traumatic brain injury remains a significant burden in the emergency department, with resource utilization—including unnecessary neurosurgical consultations and repeat CTs—being a major concern even though most injuries are mild. The modified brain injury guidelines (mBIG), which originated in the US, provide a structured approach to stratifying TBI severity to safely reduce resource use. While these US-developed pathways have shown promise in improving resource efficiency without compromising patient outcomes, their applicability and safety profile within the Australian clinical context remain unvalidated. This local assessment is crucial because current regional guidelines, such as those in NSW, may lead to high utilization of resources like neurosurgical consultations for routine findings. Therefore, understanding the local impact of adopting such modified guidelines is important for optimizing care.

dailyEditorial.title: Evaluating the TWIST score and point-of-care ultrasound for paediatric testicular torsion
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When evaluating acute scrotal pain in a child, remember that while the TWIST score and POCUS are highly accurate tools for risk stratification, the residual false-negative rate means you cannot rely on a negative score to completely exclude torsion. If the clinical suspicion remains high despite a low score, proceed with caution and consider further workup or urology consultation. The objective ultrasound findings provide strong supporting data when interpreting the score.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This study assessed the diagnostic utility of the TWIST score combined with point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) for testicular torsion in children up to 15 years old, specifically when interpreted by emergency physicians o…
dailyEditorial.summary: This study assessed the diagnostic utility of the TWIST score combined with point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) for testicular torsion in children up to 15 years old, specifically when interpreted by emergency physicians or pediatricians. The findings indicate that the TWIST score maintains high sensitivity (91%) and specificity (95%) even when performed by non-urology specialists in the emergency department setting. While the overall diagnostic accuracy is high, the authors caution that the non-negligible false-negative rate means the score should not be used to definitively rule out torsion. Furthermore, when defining positivity based on objective ultrasound findings like impaired blood flow or the whirlpool sign, the sensitivity and specificity remain robust, though the positive predictive value is lower.

dailyEditorial.title: Journal update monthly top five
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When managing septic shock, remember that while prognostic factors are actively being researched, no single factor provides a definitive cutoff for predicting high mortality. Focus on aggressive, guideline-directed resuscitation while remaining vigilant for subtle clinical deterioration, as the literature suggests risk stratification remains complex. Don't over-rely on any single biomarker or clinical score without considering the patient's overall trajectory.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This month's digest presents five high-yield articles from outside of emergency medicine, curated by South West England residents using a multimodal search approach.
dailyEditorial.summary: This month's digest presents five high-yield articles from outside of emergency medicine, curated by South West England residents using a multimodal search approach. A standout piece is a systematic review and meta-analysis focusing on prognostic factors for mortality in septic shock. The authors highlight the persistent clinical challenge of identifying patients at high risk of death despite ongoing resuscitation efforts. This review synthesizes data on various factors associated with early mortality in adults with septic shock. While the overall utility is noted, the authors emphasize that a clear, definitive risk factor analysis remains elusive, making this a 'head turner' piece rather than an immediate 'game changer.'

dailyEditorial.title: Point-of-care ultrasound by trained emergency physicians versus radiologists for deep vein thrombosis diagnosis: a prospective blinded study on diagnostic accuracy and time efficiency
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Given the high sensitivity and specificity, you can feel confident using trained ED personnel for initial DVT screening via compression ultrasound. The most impactful finding is the massive reduction in time-to-diagnosis, which could significantly improve ED throughput. Remember that while the accuracy is high, these findings suggest a need to evaluate if this efficiency translates into tangible downstream benefits, like reduced length of stay.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This prospective, blinded study compared the diagnostic utility and efficiency of point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) performed by trained emergency physicians against the established standard of care, which was radiologis…
dailyEditorial.summary: This prospective, blinded study compared the diagnostic utility and efficiency of point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) performed by trained emergency physicians against the established standard of care, which was radiologist-performed Doppler ultrasound, for suspected deep vein thrombosis (DVT). The results were quite compelling, showing that the emergency physicians achieved high diagnostic accuracy, reporting a sensitivity of 97.0% and a specificity of 99.3%. Furthermore, the time savings were dramatic, with the median time-to-diagnosis being one hour for the ED physicians compared to four hours for the radiology team. The excellent interobserver agreement, with kappa values generally above 0.90, suggests that the bedside approach is not only accurate but also significantly streamlines the diagnostic pathway for these patients.

dailyEditorial.title: Design Refinements for Sepsis Albumin Trials: Building on ICARUS-ED Experience
dailyEditorial.takeaway: The feasibility demonstrated in ICARUS-ED suggests that incorporating concentrated albumin into early sepsis protocols in the ED is logistically viable, even with mixed patient populations. While the trial confirms the ability to enroll and manage these patients, remember that the high rate of confirmed infection (95%) is impressive but doesn't negate the underlying diagnostic uncertainty of undifferentiated sepsis. Proceed with caution, recognizing this is pilot data informing future, larger trials.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: Williams et al.
dailyEditorial.summary: Williams et al. report on the ICARUS-ED pilot randomized controlled trial, which explored the use of concentrated albumin for undifferentiated sepsis management directly within the emergency department setting. The authors highlight the trial's success in establishing a feasible framework for conducting real-time sepsis trials, which is a significant logistical hurdle in this area. Notably, the study managed to enroll and maintain high protocol compliance, even incorporating ward-based patients alongside those destined for the ICU. Furthermore, they confirmed the presence of infection in a high percentage of the enrolled cohort, which is valuable given the diagnostic ambiguity inherent in early sepsis presentation. This work provides important preliminary data on optimizing resuscitation strategies in the acute ED setting.

dailyEditorial.title: Naloxone administration associated with improved survival in PEA out-of-hospital cardiac arrests
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For OHCA patients presenting in PEA, the data suggests a potential benefit to administering naloxone prehospital, as it was associated with improved survival to discharge. However, remember this is observational, so do not change your standard protocol based on this alone. Furthermore, do not anticipate a benefit for patients presenting with shockable rhythms or asystole.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This retrospective cohort study examined the link between prehospital naloxone administration and survival outcomes in patients who experienced out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA).
dailyEditorial.summary: This retrospective cohort study examined the link between prehospital naloxone administration and survival outcomes in patients who experienced out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA). The authors found a specific association: naloxone use correlated with improved survival to hospital discharge only in the subset of OHCA patients presenting in pulseless electrical activity (PEA). Notably, the intervention showed no associated benefit for patients presenting with shockable rhythms or asystole. The conclusion emphasizes that while the data suggests a benefit in PEA, the study design necessitates that future confirmation of true causality must come from prospective, randomized controlled trials.

dailyEditorial.title: Systematic Review of Pediatric Ketamine in Emergency Department Procedural Sedation: Frequency and Predictors of Adverse Events
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When considering ketamine for procedural sedation in pediatrics, remember that the literature synthesis is focused on compiling adverse event frequencies and predictors. While this review is helpful for a general overview, the clinical utility of any identified predictor variable should be interpreted cautiously due to the varied methodologies across included studies. Always maintain vigilance for signs of adverse reactions, as the pooled estimates provide a general guide rather than a definitive protocol change.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This systematic review synthesizes the existing literature regarding the use of intravenous ketamine for procedural sedation in the pediatric emergency department.
dailyEditorial.summary: This systematic review synthesizes the existing literature regarding the use of intravenous ketamine for procedural sedation in the pediatric emergency department. The primary goal was to consolidate the reported rates of various adverse events associated with this anesthetic agent in this specific population. Furthermore, the authors undertook an effort to catalogue and estimate the clinical utility of any predictor variables that have been previously identified in the literature. While the review aims to provide a comprehensive overview of safety profiles and risk stratification, the synthesis is based on pooling data from multiple studies, which is crucial for establishing reliable estimates of event frequency. Understanding these adverse event rates and potential predictors is key for optimizing sedation protocols in the pediatric ED setting.

dailyEditorial.title: Presepsin for sepsis diagnosis in emergency departments: a multicentre study
dailyEditorial.takeaway: While presepsin showed marginally better diagnostic accuracy than PCT in this cohort, remember that its utility is supportive, not definitive. Do not let a single biomarker result dictate the need for aggressive resuscitation or antibiotic escalation; always integrate these findings with the patient's clinical picture and lactate trends. The association with increased mortality is worth noting for risk stratification, but clinical judgment remains paramount.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This multicenter study assessed the utility of presepsin and procalcitonin (PCT) for diagnosing sepsis and septic shock in the emergency department setting, using the established Sepsis-3 criteria.
dailyEditorial.summary: This multicenter study assessed the utility of presepsin and procalcitonin (PCT) for diagnosing sepsis and septic shock in the emergency department setting, using the established Sepsis-3 criteria. The authors found that presepsin demonstrated slightly superior diagnostic accuracy compared to PCT for both sepsis and septic shock, as evidenced by the Area Under the ROC Curve (AUROC) values. Furthermore, elevated levels of both biomarkers were independently associated with increased 30-day mortality risk. While the predictive values are noted, the overall diagnostic accuracy reported for both markers was considered modest. The key conclusion emphasizes that these biomarkers are best utilized as adjuncts to clinical assessment rather than standalone diagnostic tools for guiding critical decisions like resuscitation or antibiotic therapy.

dailyEditorial.title: Should anticoagulants be initiated in patients with sepsis-induced new-onset atrial fibrillation? Best evidence topic report
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For patients with sepsis-induced new-onset AF, do not initiate routine anticoagulation based on current observational data, as the evidence does not support stroke prevention and one study noted increased stroke risk. Continue to weigh the benefits against the bleeding risk, but recognize that a definitive randomized trial is needed before changing standard practice. Be mindful of the potential for increased bleeding or stroke, respectively.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This topic report synthesizes the current evidence regarding the use of anticoagulation for stroke prevention in patients who develop new-onset atrial fibrillation secondary to sepsis.
dailyEditorial.summary: This topic report synthesizes the current evidence regarding the use of anticoagulation for stroke prevention in patients who develop new-onset atrial fibrillation secondary to sepsis. The review examined four observational studies, and the findings were not conclusive enough to support routine anticoagulation. Notably, none of the studies demonstrated a significant reduction in stroke risk with the initiation of anticoagulation, and one large observational cohort even suggested a paradoxical increase in stroke incidence among those receiving anticoagulants. While bleeding risk was not consistently elevated across the literature, one study did point toward a potential benefit of anticoagulation regarding overall mortality. Given these mixed and somewhat conflicting results, the authors stress that a definitive randomized controlled trial is necessary to clarify the appropriate role of anticoagulation in this high-risk, septic population.

dailyEditorial.title: Validation of The Resuscitation Management (THERM) score in the emergency department
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When stratifying risk in the resuscitation bay, remember that the THERM score integrates bicarbonate and systolic blood pressure status in a unique way. If you have the opportunity to calculate this score, it appears to offer a predictive edge over standard tools like NEWS2. However, given that this is a validation piece, treat the score as an adjunct to your clinical judgment rather than a standalone determinant for escalation.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This paper focuses on validating the Resuscitation Management (THERM) score, a specific prediction tool designed for critically ill patients presenting in the emergency department.
dailyEditorial.summary: This paper focuses on validating the Resuscitation Management (THERM) score, a specific prediction tool designed for critically ill patients presenting in the emergency department. The score is calculated using a combination of physiological parameters: adding the bicarbonate level (capped at 22 mmol/L) to the total Glasgow Coma Scale, and then subtracting 4 if the systolic blood pressure is low. The authors report that in their initial validation, THERM demonstrated significantly superior performance compared to the National Early Warning Score (NEWS), even when considering the updated NEWS2 system. This suggests that incorporating bicarbonate and specific blood pressure criteria might offer a more nuanced prognostic capability for resource allocation in the resuscitation bay.

dailyEditorial.title: A Woman With Rapidly Progressive Weakness
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When encountering acute, progressive, flaccid paralysis with a distinct sensory level in a patient with CKD, strongly consider a myelopathy or polyneuropathy rather than just a simple motor nerve issue. The combination of flaccidity, areflexia, and sensory loss below a specific dermatome mandates a focused search for underlying metabolic derangements or compressive myelopathy. Don't miss the workup for toxins or electrolyte abnormalities.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This case report details a 57-year-old woman with known chronic kidney disease who presented with a rapid, 6-hour onset of bilateral, nontraumatic lower extremity weakness and numbness.
dailyEditorial.summary: This case report details a 57-year-old woman with known chronic kidney disease who presented with a rapid, 6-hour onset of bilateral, nontraumatic lower extremity weakness and numbness. On exam, she demonstrated profound flaccid paralysis in the legs, absent reflexes, and a distinct dissociative sensory loss affecting pain and temperature below the nipple-to-xiphoid level, alongside incontinence. The presentation is highly suggestive of an acute neuromuscular process, and the authors highlight the importance of considering underlying systemic or metabolic etiologies in the setting of progressive weakness. The clinical picture warrants a thorough workup to differentiate between peripheral nerve injury, acute myelopathy, or a toxin/metabolic cause, especially given her comorbidities.

dailyEditorial.title: Methodological limitations of NOM in the WSES acute appendicitis guidelines
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When considering NOM for acute appendicitis, remember that the diagnostic yield of admission CT is imperfect, potentially missing microperforations or occult neoplasms. Given the high recurrence rate approaching 43%, these limitations suggest that while NOM is an option, it requires careful risk stratification and awareness of the diagnostic gaps inherent in the imaging modality.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This piece critically examines the methodological weaknesses surrounding the use of nonoperative management (NOM) for acute appendicitis, particularly in light of the recent 2025 WSES Guidelines that support its use for…
dailyEditorial.summary: This piece critically examines the methodological weaknesses surrounding the use of nonoperative management (NOM) for acute appendicitis, particularly in light of the recent 2025 WSES Guidelines that support its use for uncomplicated cases. The authors apply the GRADE framework and highlight that relying on admission computed tomography carries significant limitations. Specifically, CT can severely underestimate the presence of microperforations and has a documented failure rate of up to 3.2% in excluding occult appendiceal neoplasms. Given these diagnostic uncertainties and the reported recurrence rates reaching 42.6%, the article urges a more cautious approach to adopting NOM.
